title: C Write to Files
content:
### Writing to Files in C

Writing to files can be done character-by-character, as strings, or formatted.

### File Modes for Writing

| Mode | Description |
|------|-------------|
| `"w"` | Open for writing (creates/truncates) |
| `"a"` | Open for appending (creates if not exists) |
| `"wb"` | Open binary file for writing |
| `"w+"` | Open for reading and writing (creates/truncates) |
| `"a+"` | Open for reading and appending |

### Writing Functions

| Function | Writes | Use Case |
|----------|--------|----------|
| `fputc(c, fp)` | Single character | Char-by-char output |
| `fputs(str, fp)` | String | Writing strings |
| `fprintf(fp, fmt, ...)` | Formatted data | Writing with formatting |
| `fwrite(buf, size, count, fp)` | Binary data | Binary file output |

### Important Notes

- `"w"` mode overwrites the file if it exists
- `"a"` mode appends to the end of file
- Always flush or close files to ensure data is written
- Use `fflush(fp)` to force write buffered data

---

> **Key Point:** Modes matter: `"w"` truncates existing files, `"a"` appends. Use `"a"` when you want to add to an existing file without overwriting it.

quiz.question: What does mode "w" do if the file already exists?
